verdicts.reason: The task was a clean reframe, and Adobe delivered a faithful, well-centered 4:5 crop with the latte art intact, whereas Canva covered the photo's focal point with an unsolicited framed 'COFFEE LOVE / Savor the moment' text card.

Adobe produced a clean full-bleed 9:16 story with the coffee prominent and edge-to-edge, while Canva shrank the photo into a small letterboxed inset with dark dead-space borders and added an uninvited 'Sip and Savor' caption.

Both faithfully kept the product, but for a web banner Canva's expanded beige panel with the 'Skincare Routine with Nature's Best' headline reads as a finished, on-brief banner, whereas Adobe's plain crop leaves the product small with empty negative space and no messaging.

The brief explicitly required a headline, and Canva nailed it with a tight smiling-face crop plus bold 'HOW I STAY PRODUCTIVE' and 'Episode 1: Stay Focused' text, while Adobe's reframe is clean but has zero headline and therefore fails the core thumbnail requirement.

Adobe delivers a clean, faithful 4:5 reframe with the latte cup well centered, whereas Canva slapped an unrequested paper-card overlay ('COFFEE LOVE / Savor the moment') over the photo, obscuring the subject the brief asked to showcase.

Adobe gives a clean full-bleed 9:16 vertical crop of the coffee, while Canva letterboxes the image inside a dark bordered frame and adds unsolicited 'Sip and Savor' text, wasting story real estate and reducing it to a small inset.

Both are usable 16:9 banners, but Adobe's full-bleed product-on-sand crop is cleaner and faithful, whereas Canva added an unrequested beige copy panel ('Skincare Routine with Nature's Best') that is polished yet introduces marketing text the brief did not ask for.

This task explicitly requires a headline and Adobe produced a clean crop with NO text at all, failing the core requirement, while Canva delivered a punchy tight-cropped thumbnail with a clear 'HOW I STAY PRODUCTIVE' headline plus episode label exactly as a YouTube thumbnail demands.

Adobe delivers a clean, faithful 4:5 reframe with the latte well-centered, while Canva covered the actual coffee subject with an unsolicited "Savor the moment" text card that the brief never asked for.

Adobe fills the 9:16 frame with a crisp, well-composed vertical crop, whereas Canva shrank the photo into a small inset on a dark background with an unsolicited "Sip and Savor" caption, wasting the canvas and making the subject read tiny.

Adobe's wide reframe is faithful but flat with the product dead-center and dead texture space, while Canva added unsolicited copy yet produced a more banner-like layout with a headline and breathing room — strong on purpose-fit but penalized for inventing text.

The brief explicitly requires a headline, and Adobe's clean crop has no text at all so it fails the core spec, while Canva delivers a punchy, legible YouTube thumbnail with a tight face crop and a bold "HOW I STAY PRODUCTIVE" headline.
